one_line_takeaway: Training a linear probe on each transformer layer's averaged activations turns white-box internal states into a markedly stronger membership-inference signal than output-based attacks (avg ~15.7% AUC gain, AUC>0.6 in 65% of unimodal / 86% of multimodal cases), and pinpoints which layers leak the most.
key_finding: Reading internal activations substantially improves MIA detectability over output-based attacks, and the membership signal is concentrated at specific layers that differ by bias type. Unimodally, LUMIA beats prior methods on 174 of 176 cases (98.86%) with an average AUC gain of ~15.7%, and crosses the AUC>0.6 'successful attack' threshold in 65.33% of cases versus 44.5% for SOTA (a 46.80% relative increase). Multimodally (no prior baseline exists), 85.9% of experiments exceed AUC>0.6, and visual inputs contribute meaningful membership signal. Layer analysis: for Pythia, TB datasets peak at early layers (~3-5) while NGB datasets peak at deeper layers (~15-18); GPT-Neo is always most detectable at deep layers (~25); multimodal signal peaks in middle layers (visual ~8-9 or 15; visual+text ~12-13). TB datasets are consistently easier to attack than NGB datasets even at high overlap.
core_contribution: LUMIA proposes using Linear Probes (lightweight MLP classifiers trained on a model's internal per-layer activations) to detect and assess Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) against LLMs and Large Multimodal Models. Unlike prior black-/grey-box MIAs that threshold the model's output (loss, logits, min-k% probability), LUMIA is a white-box method: it places a hook after each transformer layer, averages the activation vector over all tokens of an input (Eq. 1: A_l(x_i) = mean over tokens of a_l(t_j)), and trains one probe per layer to classify member vs. non-member, then reports the AUC per layer and the layer l* of maximum AUC. The paper's own distinguishing contributions (vs. the closest prior white-box probing work, Liu et al. [17]) are: (1) a full layer-by-layer analysis rather than probing a single layer; (2) no fine-tuning of the target model and no fixed input prompt (Liu et al. fine-tuned proxy models with a standardized prompt, which the authors argue biases results and narrows the search space); (3) first extension of activation-based MIA to multimodal (image+text) models, probing both the visual encoder and the visual+text LLM; and (4) systematic study across model size, dataset nature, N-gram bias (NGB) vs. temporal bias (TB), and deduplicated vs. non-deduplicated model versions.
threat_model: The adversary (or an AI auditor) seeks to determine whether a specific text or image+text sample was part of an LLM's pre-training set (a membership inference / privacy-leakage threat relevant to copyright and sensitive-data auditing). LUMIA assumes a white-box setting: the attacker can run forward passes and read internal hidden-state activations of the target model, and possesses a set of labeled member/non-member samples on which to train the linear probes (80/20 train-val split, 3 repetitions). This is a stronger access assumption than the grey-/black-box baselines it compares against, which only need the model's output. The paper frames white-box access as justified by the need for transparent AI audits, citing Casper et al. [6] that black-box access is insufficient for rigorous audits.
limitations: The two reported average-gain figures are slightly inconsistent (abstract/Takeaways say 15.71%; Section 5.1 text says 15.75%). The method requires genuine white-box activation access plus a labeled member/non-member training set, a strong assumption that limits real-world attacker applicability and makes comparison to output-only baselines somewhat asymmetric. Absolute NGB AUCs remain modest (many in the 0.55-0.60 range, e.g. GPT-Neo N=13/P=0.8 fails to reach 0.6 except on Github), so 'improvement' is often over near-random baselines. The paper does not resolve the foundational objection (Zhang et al. [34], which it cites) that MIA success may not prove training-set membership, and Das et al. [7] blind heuristics remain very competitive on TB sets (e.g. Heuristics reaches 0.987 on WikiMIA vs LUMIA 0.99). Multimodal results use only one model family (LLaVA-OneVision, already deduplicated, so no dedup comparison possible), and the Magpie black-image workaround is a somewhat artificial setup. Only pre-training membership is studied; no defense is implemented (noise-injection into informative layers is left as future work).

datasets_used: Temporal ArXiv and Temporal wiki (unimodal, TB)
Mimir subsets of The Pile: Wikipedia, Github, Pubmed, Pile-CC, ArXiv, DM_math, Hackernews (unimodal, NGB)
quantitative_results: Unimodal average AUC improvement over prior techniques: 15.71% (abstract/Takeaways) / 15.75% (Section 5.1)
LUMIA beats prior methods on 174 of 176 unimodal cases (98.86%)
AUC>0.6 reached in 65.33% of unimodal cases vs 44.5% for SOTA (46.80% relative increase)
Gutenberg AUC 0.98; ArXiv-1-month 0.93; Temporal wiki 0.93; Temporal ArXiv 0.86
ArXiv-CS 0.842 (+25.11% over Liu et al.); ArXiv-Math 0.646 (+12.54%, hardest text)
Multimodal best AUCs: ScienceQA 0.990 (7B, textual+visual), IconQA 0.903 (7B), Magpie only 0.572 (text-only)
NGB (Table 2) avg improvements over Duan et al.: N=13/P=0.8 -> 11.96%, N=13/P=0.2 -> 13.93%, N=7/P=0.2 -> 17.03%
NGB AUCs remain modest (~0.55-0.70); DM_math ref-baseline collapses to 0.44/0.41 giving +68%/+129% headline gains
Model-size trend: larger Pythia/GPT-Neo generally raises AUC (better in 85.9% of cases); ArXiv-1-month dips at 12B (0.92 -> 0.84 dedup / 0.86 non-dedup)
